title: Talk to the Machine: Prompt Engineering

modules.title: Why Your First Prompt Always Fails
modules.sub: Diagnosing what goes wrong before you even start
modules.description: Learners step into the role of an AI prompt consultant and immediately hit a wall: their clients' first attempts at prompting are producing useless results. They analyse real failure cases to identify exactly why vague, context-free prompts break down, building a mental model of what AI actually needs to respond well.

modules.title: Roles, Rules, and Constraints That Work
modules.sub: Giving the AI a job, boundaries, and a clear brief
modules.description: Learners experiment with assigning specific roles to the AI and layering in constraints and output rules to steer its responses for each client's unique needs. They compare outputs across different prompt structures, deciding which combinations of role, rules, and format produce the most reliable and useful results.

modules.title: Chaining Prompts to Solve Hard Problems
modules.sub: Breaking big questions into a sequence of smart prompts
modules.description: Learners tackle their clients' most complex requests by splitting them into a chain of connected prompts, where each output feeds into the next. They plan, test, and refine multi-step prompt sequences, learning how to keep the AI on track across several exchanges rather than expecting one prompt to do everything.

modules.title: Design a Prompt That Teaches Someone Else
modules.sub: Turning your prompt expertise into a resource for others
modules.description: Learners create an annotated prompt guide for one of their three clients, explaining every design choice so that someone else could use and adapt it. This capstone task pushes them to articulate the reasoning behind role assignment, constraints, and chaining in plain language, cementing their understanding by teaching it.

synopsis: You've been hired as an AI prompt consultant for three very different clients — a musician, a scientist, and a city planner — and none of their AI tools are doing what they need yet. You have to diagnose why and fix it, one prompt at a time.
rationale: Prompt engineering is rapidly becoming a foundational workplace skill, yet teens are mostly using AI reactively rather than learning to direct it with precision and intentionality.
learning_outcome: Students can write, iterate, and chain prompts using role assignment, constraints, and output formatting to reliably extract useful, accurate results from a general-purpose AI tool.
source_signals: Modern-Day Oracles or Bullshit Machines? How to thrive in a ChatGPT world
How to learn AI for students & professionals (guide)
